title: Your First Program
summary: Write your very first line of Python and make the computer say hello to the world.

blocks.md:
Every programmer in history has started exactly where you are right now. The very first program almost everyone writes is called **Hello, World!** — and it is a tradition going back to the 1970s.

Your goal in this lesson: make the computer display a message on the screen. That is it. One line of code. Let's go.

Python has a built-in command called `print()`. Think of it like a megaphone — whatever you put inside the parentheses, Python will shout it out onto your screen.

The parentheses `()` are how you hand something *to* a command. Everything inside is the message you want to display.

blocks.title: print() is like a megaphone
blocks.md: Imagine handing a note to a friend and saying "read this out loud." `print()` is that friend. You put your message inside the parentheses, and Python reads it out loud — onto your screen.

blocks.caption: The classic first program. One line, and it works!

blocks.md:
When Python runs this line, it looks inside the parentheses, finds your message, and displays it. The output appears instantly below your code.

You just wrote a real, working Python program. Take a second to appreciate that.

The quotes around `"Hello, World!"` tell Python: *this is text, not a command*. In Python, a piece of text wrapped in quotes is called a **string**.

Without quotes, Python would think `Hello` is the name of something it should look up — and it would get confused and show an error.

blocks.title: Forgetting the quotes causes an error
blocks.md:
```python
print(Hello, World!)
```
This will **crash** with a `SyntaxError`. Python sees `Hello` and `World` as unknown names, not text. Always wrap your text in quotes.

blocks.caption: Single quotes and double quotes are both fine for strings.

blocks.md: Python accepts both `'single quotes'` and `"double quotes"` around strings. The output is identical. Most people pick one style and stick with it — but you can use either. Just make sure your opening and closing quote match!
You can print more than one item in a single `print()` call by separating them with a **comma**. Python will put a space between each item automatically.

blocks.caption: Commas separate items; Python adds a space between them.

blocks.title: Print as many items as you like
blocks.md: There is no limit to how many items you can pass to `print()`. Each comma-separated value gets printed on the same line, separated by a single space. Great for mixing text and values later on!

blocks.code:
print("Hello, World!")
print("I am learning Python.")
print("This is awesome!")
blocks.output:
Hello, World!
I am learning Python.
This is awesome!
blocks.caption: Each print() call starts on a new line.

blocks.md: Every `print()` call automatically ends with a newline, so the next `print()` always starts on a fresh line. You can stack as many as you need.

blocks.question:
What will this code print?

print("Python", "is", "fun")
options: Error — you can't pass multiple items to print()
blocks.explanation: When you separate items with commas inside print(), Python joins them with a single space. So "Python", "is", "fun" becomes Python is fun.

keyTakeaways: `print()` displays text (and other values) on the screen.
Text in Python must be wrapped in quotes — either single `'...'` or double `"..."` — to be treated as a string.
Separate multiple items with commas inside `print()` and Python adds spaces between them automatically.
Each `print()` call outputs on its own line.
You just wrote real Python code — every expert started exactly here.

exercise.prompt: Write a small program that introduces yourself. It should print three lines: your name, what you are learning, and one thing you are excited about. Use at least one `print()` call that has two items separated by a comma.
exercise.starterCode:
# Replace the blanks with your own words!
print("My name is", "___")
print("___")
print("___")
exercise.hint: Remember: text goes inside quotes. Use a comma inside print() to join two pieces of text with a space between them.
exercise.solution:
print("My name is", "Alex")
print("I am learning Python.")
print("I am excited to build my own programs!")
